Beale documents that it contains no bony structure in the forward section, making it a resilient, compressible ram. This anatomy is the factual basis for historical accounts of sperm whales sinking ships by head-on collision.

candidates.anchor: sending forth beautiful crystalline shoots, as when the first thin delicate ice is just forming in water
candidates.note: Spermaceti is liquid inside a living whale but solidifies on contact with air, forming waxy, pearlescent crystals. Beale describes the crystallization process in detail, noting that cooled spermaceti was the basis for the finest candles of the era — far whiter and less smoky than tallow — which is why the sperm whale was so commercially prized.

candidates.anchor: the great Heidelburgh Tun of the Sperm Whale
candidates.note: The whale's oil chamber is compared to the Heidelberg Tun, a huge wine cask at Heidelberg Castle. The joke makes anatomy memorable through the scale of storage and drinking.

candidates.anchor: Baling of the Case
candidates.note: The whale's head becomes a huge container, compared to the Heidelberg Tun. The joke helps students picture scale through a human-made object.

candidates.anchor: Lavater not only treats of the various faces of men, but also attentively studies the faces of horses
candidates.note: Johann Caspar Lavater (1741–1801) was a Swiss pastor whose Essays on Physiognomy (1775–78) argued that character and intelligence could be read from facial structure. His method—applied here to the whale—was enormously popular but unscientific; by Melville's era it was already being discredited alongside phrenology.

candidates.anchor: Nor have Gall and his disciple Spurzheim failed to throw out some hints touching the phrenological characteristics of other beings than man.
candidates.note: The phrenology reference points to a now-discredited nineteenth-century reading system. Ishmael borrows its language while making whale interpretation look absurdly difficult.

candidates.unit_title: The Honor and Glory of Whaling
candidates.anchor: that fish, flesh, and fowl idol of the Philistines, Dagon by name; who being planted before the ark of Israel, his horse’s head and both the palms of his hands fell off from him
candidates.note: In 1 Samuel 5:1–5, the Philistines place the captured Ark of the Covenant in the temple of their fish-god Dagon; the idol is found fallen and broken before the Ark. Melville uses the story to argue that Dagon — a fish deity — was really a whale-god, and that when the Ark defeated Dagon, the 'whaleman' god was toppled by Israel.

candidates.anchor: A penny roll would choke him
candidates.note: Right whales are filter feeders whose throat is surprisingly narrow, too small to swallow a human. Whalers knew this, and the fisherman's saying mocks the old Sag-Harbor skeptic's literal worry: if Jonah's whale had two spouts, making it a right whale, it could not have swallowed the prophet whole.

candidates.anchor: Arion and the dolphin
candidates.note: Arion was a legendary Greek lyric poet who, threatened by sailors intent on robbing him, leaped overboard and was carried safely to shore by a dolphin charmed by his music. Melville pairs the story with Jonah and the whale as sea-creature rescue tales that skeptics doubted, arguing that doubt alone does not disprove them.

candidates.anchor: two spouts in his head
candidates.note: Ishmael tests the Jonah story against whalemen's anatomical knowledge. The chapter stages a debate between scripture, observation, and comic literalism.

candidates.anchor: a couple of whist-tables
candidates.note: Melville treats Jonah's story with comic literalism: the prophet's lodging inside the whale becomes a problem of furniture and space.

candidates.anchor: the entire spear is some ten or twelve feet in length; the staff is much slighter than that of the harpoon
candidates.note: The pitchpole lance was a lighter, longer weapon than the harpoon, designed for throwing at distance rather than penetrating and holding. Whalers used it only after the harpoon had secured the whale, as its slender pine shaft could not withstand the strain of a running line.

candidates.anchor: So in dreams, have I seen majestic Satan thrusting forth his tormented colossal claw from the flame Baltic of Hell
candidates.note: The image of Satan reaching a colossal claw out of a fiery hell echoes Milton's Paradise Lost, where the fallen archangel rears up from the burning lake. Melville offers this Miltonic vision as the dark counterpart to a serene, Isaiah-inspired view of the whale's tail, two opposed literary lenses for the same sublime sight.

candidates.anchor: Paul in Corinthians, about corruption and incorruption
candidates.note: 1 Corinthians 15:42-43 reads: "It is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption: It is sown in dishonour; it is raised in glory." Paul is describing the resurrection of the body. Melville applies this to ambergris found incorrupt inside a rotting whale carcass, turning a whaleman's commodity into a theological paradox.

candidates.anchor: Greek fire
candidates.note: Greek fire was a historical incendiary weapon. The comparison makes the try-works blaze feel military, frightening, and hard to control.

candidates.anchor: the purest of oil, in its unmanufactured, and, therefore, unvitiated state; a fluid unknown to solar, lunar, or astral contrivances ashore
candidates.note: Spermaceti oil, drawn directly from the whale's case and tried out at sea, burned cleaner and brighter than any shore-refined lamp oil. Ashore, solar, lunar, and astral were trade names for particular oil-burning lamp designs. Melville's point: whalemen enjoy a purer, more direct light than any refined product available to landsmen.

candidates.anchor: Aladdin’s lamp
candidates.note: The Aladdin allusion turns whale oil into magical domestic comfort. It helps readers see the strange intimacy of living by the product of the hunt.

candidates.anchor: Oh! the metempsychosis! Oh! Pythagoras
candidates.note: Metempsychosis, the transmigration of souls through successive bodies, was the central doctrine of Pythagoras (c. 570-495 BC), who taught that a soul might pass from human to animal to human across lives. Melville invokes it humorously: the endless cycle of catching and processing whales mirrors a soul doomed to repeat the same life.

candidates.anchor: ankers of Geneva
candidates.note: An anker is a cask holding about ten gallons; Geneva is an early English word for Dutch genever (gin), from the juniper berry used to flavor it. The Dutch provision list thereby records thousands of gallons of gin for the whaling fleet, underscoring the satirical point about polar fishermen's drinking.

candidates.anchor: the convivial natives pledge each other in bumpers of train oil
candidates.note: Train oil is rendered blubber oil from whales or seals, the primary fuel and trade commodity of Arctic peoples. Scoresby describes Arctic communities sustaining themselves on whale and seal products including oil used as food. Melville contrasts Arctic native subsistence with Dutch commercial excess.

candidates.anchor: the privilege of Jonah alone; the privilege of discoursing upon the joists and beams
candidates.note: In the Book of Jonah (1-2), the prophet is swallowed by a great fish and lives inside it three days before being cast up. Melville treats this as conferring on Jonah unique authority to describe the whale's interior. The joke is that Ishmael must establish a competing credential to claim the same anatomical knowledge.

candidates.anchor: the hair-hung sword that so affrighted Damocles
candidates.note: The whale jaw hangs like Damocles' sword, a sign of danger suspended over power. The image makes the temple scene feel precarious.

candidates.anchor: Captain Scoresby’s estimate, of seventy tons for the largest sized Greenland whale
candidates.note: William Scoresby Jr.'s Account of the Arctic Regions (1820) is the source Melville cites here. Scoresby gave detailed weight estimates for the bowhead (Greenland) whale based on actual measurements. Melville then extrapolates upward to estimate a sperm whale at ninety tons, using Scoresby's figure as his scientific baseline.

candidates.anchor: Cuvier pronounced these fragments to have belonged to some utterly unknown Leviathanic species
candidates.note: Georges Cuvier (1769-1832), the French naturalist who founded vertebrate paleontology, was the first scientific authority to classify whale fossils as a distinct group. His pronouncements carried enormous weight; Melville invokes his name to lend geological authority to the meditation on the whale's deep antiquity.

candidates.anchor: Here Saturn’s grey chaos rolls over me
candidates.note: In Greek-Roman myth, Saturn (Kronos) reigned over the primeval Golden Age before Jupiter overthrew him. Bulfinch describes the pre-Olympian era as a formless time. Melville invokes Saturn's chaos to evoke a period before human history, geological deep time when the whale alone ruled the seas.

candidates.anchor: I have invariably used a huge quarto edition of Johnson
candidates.note: Samuel Johnson (1709-1784) compiled A Dictionary of the English Language (1755), the standard English dictionary for a century. Ishmael's joke is that Johnson's own bulk makes his huge quarto a fitting authority for so vast a subject as the whale, a massive lexicographer to define a massive animal.

candidates.anchor: Owen, the English Anatomist, it turned out that this alleged reptile was a whale
candidates.note: Sir Richard Owen (1804-1892) examined Basilosaurus bones sent from Alabama and confirmed in 1839 that the creature was a whale, not a reptile, proposing the name Zeuglodon (yoke-tooth). Melville uses this real scientific episode to dramatize the theme that whale anatomy resists even expert classification.

candidates.anchor: Applied to any other creature than the Leviathan⁠—to an ant or a flea⁠—such portly terms might justly be deemed unwarrantably grandiloquent.
candidates.note: Large words feel ridiculous on small creatures but suitable for Leviathan. Ishmael uses the size of language to match the size of whales.

candidates.anchor: Basilosaurus
candidates.note: Basilosaurus is an extinct primitive whale genus. The fossil name helps Ishmael show how whale bones can strain systems of classification.

candidates.unit_title: Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?
candidates.anchor: hunted for thousands of years, by Semiramis, by Porus, by Hannibal
candidates.note: These names stand for ancient commanders who fielded war elephants: Hannibal, the Carthaginian general, most famously crossed the Alps in 218 BCE with an army that included at least 37 elephants to invade Rome. Melville lists such elephant-hunters across millennia to argue that elephants have survived long hunting, so the whale will too.

candidates.anchor: multum in parvo , Sheffield contrivances
candidates.note: Multum in parvo is a Latin phrase meaning much in little. Sheffield was England's cutlery capital, famous for multi-blade folding knives. Melville uses both the Latin tag and the Sheffield reference to describe the carpenter as a compact, multi-purpose tool in human form, versatile but devoid of individual soul.

candidates.anchor: bowing your head to Pan
candidates.note: Pan is the Greek god of wild nature, pasturelands, and the universal life-force; his name was popularly connected with the Greek word for all. Melville closes the paragraph by making the serene, mysterious Pacific the domain of Pan, a wild natural sublimity that overpowers Ahab's singular obsession.

candidates.anchor: Potters’ Fields
candidates.note: The Pacific becomes a burial ground, not only a peaceful ocean. The biblical phrase qualifies the chapter's calm with graveyard imagery.

candidates.anchor: boxes of lucifers forward
candidates.note: Lucifers were friction matches, commercially available from the late 1820s onward, named for the Latin word for light-bearer. Flask's analogy pairs gunpowder with matches: the Pequod is loaded at both ends with things that kill by fire, a running metaphor for a voyage driven by Ahab's consuming rage.

candidates.anchor: one morning shortly after breakfast
candidates.note: Ahab turns the deck into a stage for command. Money, ritual, and repeated speech redirect the crew's ordinary labor toward his private revenge.

candidates.anchor: If they but knew it, almost all men in their degree, some time or other, cherish very nearly the same feelings towards the ocean with me.
candidates.note: Ishmael turns his private pull toward water into a shared human impulse. The opening is personal, but it immediately asks readers to recognize the feeling in themselves.

candidates.anchor: the Tyre of this Carthage
candidates.note: The comparison makes New Bedford and Nantucket feel larger than ordinary towns. Ishmael borrows the scale of old trading empires to describe a whaling economy.

candidates.anchor: Regent Street is not unknown to Lascars and Malays
candidates.note: New Bedford is provincial and international at the same time. Ishmael uses the street scene to show whaling as a global trade, not a local curiosity.

candidates.anchor: touching Tophet
candidates.note: Tophet was a place in the valley of Hinnom near Jerusalem where children were burned in sacrifice (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31), so accursed by the prophets that its name became a common word for hell. Among the gallows-shaped sign, the black pots, and the landlady named Coffin, Tophet is the darkest of the death-omens Ishmael half-jokingly reads into the inn.

candidates.anchor: I had not a little relied upon Queequeg’s sagacity to point out the whaler best fitted to carry us and our fortunes securely.
candidates.note: Ishmael trusts Queequeg's practical judgment when choosing a ship. Their friendship is becoming a working partnership inside the whaling world.

candidates.anchor: great activity aboard the Pequod
candidates.note: The Pequod is a workplace before it is a symbol. Loading, preparing, and managing the ship make Ahab's later quest depend on ordinary labor.

candidates.anchor: preparations were hurrying to a close
candidates.note: The chapter turns departure into logistics: sails, canvas, rigging, and stores make the adventure possible before anyone romanticizes it.

candidates.anchor: macassar oil
candidates.note: Macassar oil was a fashionable Victorian hair-dressing, marketed as coming from Makassar in the East Indies and sold widely in Britain from the early 1800s. Ishmael ranks it among the lesser oils unworthy of a royal head, part of the comic case that only sperm oil is fine enough for a coronation.

candidates.anchor: a living thump and a dead thump
candidates.note: Stubb's joke turns pain into a theory of force. He claims that a blow from a living will feels different from a blow by an object.

candidates.anchor: large wrinkled roll of yellowish sea charts
candidates.note: Ahab's hunt is also a paper-and-data project. The charts show obsession turning practical navigation into a private system.

candidates.anchor: Season-on-the-Line
candidates.note: The phrase treats the hunt as something that can be timed by migratory pattern. Ahab's obsession depends on practical whaling knowledge as well as fury.

candidates.anchor: Among whale-wise people it has often been argued whether
candidates.note: The passage frames a practical whaling debate: whether a captain should risk himself in the boat. Ahab's obsession is also professionally dangerous.

candidates.anchor: peculiar usages of whaling-vessels
candidates.note: A gam is a technical whaling custom, but it also becomes a storytelling device. Encounters between ships carry news, rumor, warning, and moral pressure.

candidates.anchor: Et tu Brute
candidates.note: Et tu, Brute, meaning and you, Brutus, are the dying words of Caesar in Shakespeare's Julius Caesar (3.1), the cry of a man stabbed by a trusted friend. Ishmael puts them in the mouth of a calf's head staring up at the diner who eats its brain, turning the meal into a grim act of self-betrayal against one's own kind.

candidates.anchor: that blood-dripping head hung to the Pequod ’s waist like the giant Holofernes’s from the girdle of Judith
candidates.note: In the Book of Judith (in the Catholic/Orthodox Bible; apocryphal in Protestant editions), the Hebrew widow Judith enters the camp of the Assyrian general Holofernes, beheads him while he sleeps, and carries his severed head back in a bag. Melville's simile links Ahab's severed whale-head to that act of gruesome deliverance — suggesting danger, triumph, and a kind of sacred violence.

candidates.anchor: the Queen be respectfully presented with the tail
candidates.note: The joke depends on royal privilege rather than whale anatomy. Ishmael turns a legal custom into comic ownership of a whale's body.

candidates.anchor: It is as if from the open field a brickkiln were transported to her planks.
candidates.note: The brickkiln image makes the Pequod feel like a factory at sea. Whaling turns the ship itself into an industrial furnace.

candidates.anchor: The timbers beneath are of a peculiar strength, fitted to sustain the weight of an almost solid mass of brick and mortar, some ten feet by eight square, and five in height.
candidates.note: The try-works are built into the Pequod's frame. The ship does not merely carry industry; it has been reinforced to become a floating factory.

candidates.anchor: barbaric vertù
candidates.note: Vertù is an Italian-derived English word for curiosities, art objects, and rare crafted items prized by collectors. King Tranquo's love of barbaric vertù marks him as a Pacific counterpart to the European virtuoso or cabinet-of-curiosities collector, casting the whale skeleton as his grandest specimen.

candidates.anchor: Thy shrunk voice sounds too calmly, sanely woeful to me.
candidates.note: Ahab finds Perth's calm sorrow harder to bear than rage. The line shows how Ahab prefers madness and force to ordinary human grief.

candidates.anchor: Emblazonings, as of crowned Babylonian kings and queens, reigned over everything
candidates.note: Babylon was the biblical symbol of supreme worldly splendor: its golden regalia and jeweled throne-rooms recur in Daniel, Isaiah, and Revelation. The morning sea blazing gold after the typhoon is given the grandeur of the ancient world's most magnificent empire, an ironic magnificence just before Ahab finds the compasses wrecked.

candidates.anchor: the devious-cruising Rachel
candidates.note: The Rachel is still searching for its own lost crew. Ishmael's rescue arrives inside another ship's grief, not as a simple happy ending.

candidates.anchor: good laugh is a mighty good thing
candidates.note: The breakfast scene turns dangerous-looking whalers into awkward hotel guests. Melville deflates heroic sea romance by showing ordinary social embarrassment.

candidates.anchor: He had been a sailor and a harpooneer in his youth, but for many years past had dedicated his life to the ministry.
candidates.note: Mapple's past as sailor and harpooneer lets the pulpit speak the language of the sea. His sermon belongs to whaling culture, not outside it.

candidates.anchor:
midships!”

 There was a low rumbling of heavy sea-boots among the benches, and a still slighter shuffling of women’s shoes, and all was quiet again, and every eye on the preacher.
candidates.note: The congregation sounds like a ship's crew before Mapple fully begins. Melville lets church space and ship space overlap.

candidates.anchor: I am the architect, not the builder
candidates.note: The architect image admits that Ishmael's classification will remain unfinished. The chapter is comic, but it also shows the novel trying to organize a subject too large to master.

candidates.anchor: The envious billows sidelong swell to whelm my track; let them; but first I pass.
candidates.note: Ahab's soliloquy turns the sea into opposition. The voice is theatrical because he speaks as if even the waves are audience and enemy.

candidates.anchor: But he drilled deep down, and blasted all my reason out of me!
candidates.note: Starbuck names the damage Ahab has done to his judgment. The short stage-like chapter lets conscience speak before obedience resumes.

candidates.anchor: I heard not all his talk with Starbuck; but to my poor eye Starbuck then looked something as I the other evening felt.
candidates.note: Stubb half-understands Starbuck's disturbance but turns it into comic fatalism. The sequence shows each officer processing Ahab differently.

candidates.anchor: Oh, boys, don’t be sentimental; it’s bad for the digestion!
candidates.note: After Ahab's quarter-deck ritual, the forecastle breaks into many voices: jokes, songs, appetite, fear, and bravado. The form keeps the crew from becoming a single obedient chorus.

candidates.anchor: all singing in chorus
candidates.note: Melville writes the forecastle scene like a play, with named speakers and stage directions. The form lets the crew's humor, fear, and fatigue arrive as a public chorus.

candidates.anchor: For one of them may have received a transfer of letters from some third, and now far remote vessel; and some of those letters may be for the people of the ship she now meets.
candidates.note: Letters make the gam more than a technical custom. A ship meeting can carry family news, delayed messages, and fragile contact with shore.

candidates.anchor: It was not very long after speaking the Goney that another homeward-bound whaleman, the Town-Ho , 9 was encountered.
candidates.note: The Town-Ho enters as another ship met at sea, but the meeting opens into a nested story. Melville uses ship-to-ship contact to move rumor, warning, and narrative across the ocean.

candidates.anchor: the secret part of the tragedy
candidates.note: The Town-Ho story is told inside another scene, with audience interruptions. The frame shows how rumor, secrecy, and performance shape what counts as truth at sea.

candidates.anchor: So the signal was set to see what response would be made.
candidates.note: The signal prepares the meeting before anyone speaks. Ship-to-ship code makes warning, illness, and rumor part of the scene's first meaning.

candidates.anchor: While yet some distance from the Pequod , she rounded to, and dropping a boat, her captain was impelled towards us, impatiently standing in the bows instead of the stern.
candidates.note: The Virgin's captain approaches impatiently, so the gam becomes comic before it becomes competitive. Ship meetings quickly become tests of pride.

candidates.unit_title: Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?
candidates.anchor: whether, in the long course of his generations
candidates.note: Ishmael asks whether whales can diminish or disappear. For modern readers, the question connects nineteenth-century whaling to later conservation concerns.

candidates.anchor: main-top-sail yard
candidates.note: This tiny chapter works almost like a stage flash. Its brevity makes the storm feel immediate instead of reported from a distance.

candidates.anchor: Nathaniel Hawthorne
candidates.note: The dedication frames the book as an act of admiration before the story begins. That matters because the novel opens with literary kinship as well as whaling subject matter.

candidates.anchor: whenever it is a damp, drizzly November in my soul
candidates.note: Ishmael describes going to sea as a practical response to despair and anger. The comedy matters, but the opening is also direct about mental strain.

candidates.anchor: Who ain’t a slave? Tell me that
candidates.note: When Moby-Dick appeared in 1851, the United States census already counted more than three million people held as property, a figure that neared four million by 1860. Ishmael stretches slavery into a universal figure for anyone who takes orders, which can make a real and specific atrocity sound like everyone's ordinary lot. The line reads as period rhetoric, not a moral verdict the book has settled.

candidates.anchor: In thoroughfares nigh the docks, any considerable seaport will frequently offer to view the queerest looking nondescripts from foreign parts.
candidates.note: The New Bedford street scene shows whaling as a global trade before the voyage begins. Ishmael sees the port through the people and goods drawn there by the fishery.

candidates.anchor: second flowering youth
candidates.note: Father Mapple's age is described as renewed rather than simply weakened. That helps explain why his authority feels weathered and forceful at once.

candidates.anchor: never cringed and never had had a creditor
candidates.note: Ishmael links Queequeg's dignity to freedom from debt and social shame. That is part of why Queequeg feels morally impressive to him.

candidates.anchor: Through all his unearthly tattooings
candidates.note: Ishmael begins to read Queequeg's tattoos as signs of character and history, not just as frightening decoration.

candidates.anchor: The more so, I say, because truly to enjoy bodily warmth, some small part of you must be cold, for there is no quality in this world that is not what it is merely by contrast.
candidates.note: Ishmael turns bodily comfort into a theory of contrast: warmth is felt because some cold remains. The shared bed becomes philosophical, not only comic.

candidates.anchor: wicked world in all meridians
candidates.note: Queequeg's reaction turns Ishmael's joke back against the Christian world he has seen. The moment shows why Queequeg cannot simply absorb the culture around him.

candidates.anchor: The owners of his ship, it seems, had lent him one, in which to carry his heavy chest to his boarding house.
candidates.note: The wheelbarrow story turns cultural misunderstanding into comedy without making Queequeg foolish. Ishmael has to learn that context changes meaning.

candidates.anchor: Try Pots
candidates.note: The inn name points to try-pots, the kettles used to render whale blubber into oil. Even dinner is framed by the labor of whaling.

candidates.anchor: now extinct as the ancient Medes
candidates.note: The Medes were a people of ancient Iran who recur through the Old Testament, in Daniel, Esther, and Isaiah, often paired with the Persians as a once-great empire long since vanished. Melville reaches for them as a scriptural byword for a nation reduced to nothing, pressing the point that the Pequot people have been wiped out just as utterly.

candidates.anchor: Yojo earnestly enjoined
candidates.note: Queequeg's god is treated as an authority in a real decision, not just comic decoration. Ishmael lets his friend's judgment shape the voyage.

candidates.anchor: Queequeg thought he knew what he was about, I suppose; he seemed to be content; and there let him rest.
candidates.note: Ishmael tries to leave Queequeg's ritual alone even while he misunderstands it. The comedy comes from friendship, impatience, and limited knowledge at once.

candidates.anchor: Towards evening, when I felt assured that all his performances and rituals must be over, I went up to his room and knocked at the door; but no answer.
candidates.note: The unanswered door turns Ishmael's curiosity into worry. His friendship with Queequeg is comic, but it is also emotionally serious.

candidates.anchor:
Muster ’em aft here⁠—blast ’em!”

 “No need of profane words, however great the hurry, Peleg,” said Bildad, “but away with thee, friend Starbuck, and do our bidding.”

 How now!
candidates.note: Peleg and Bildad's voices clash even in departure. Urgency, profanity, piety, and command all crowd the deck as the voyage begins.

candidates.anchor: short, cold Christmas
candidates.note: The Christmas departure is cold and worklike rather than cozy. Melville makes the voyage feel like labor before it feels like adventure.

candidates.anchor: Let me only say that it fared with him as with the storm-tossed ship, that miserably drives along the leeward land.
candidates.note: Bulkington's image turns seamanship into a moral choice: the safe-looking shore can be more dangerous than the open sea.

candidates.anchor: (Sperm Whale Fishery) to his visiting card, such a procedure would be deemed preeminently presuming and ridiculous.
candidates.note: Ishmael knows whaling is socially dismissed, so he argues for its dignity with comic defensiveness. The chapter is advocacy, not neutral description.

candidates.anchor: But butchers, also, and butchers of the bloodiest badge have been all Martial Commanders whom the world invariably delights to honor.
candidates.note: The comparison to celebrated butchers exposes a double standard. Ishmael asks why some forms of violence receive honor while whaling receives contempt.

candidates.anchor: There is a saltcellar of state, so called, and there may be a castor of state.
candidates.note: The royal table joke extends Ishmael's defense of whaling into absurd ceremony. He wants whale oil and whale labor recognized inside high culture.

candidates.anchor: Only some thirty arid summers had he seen; those summers had dried up all his physical superfluousness.
candidates.note: The arid-summer image turns Starbuck into a figure of discipline and restraint. That dryness matters when he later becomes Ahab's strongest conscience.

candidates.anchor: Ahasuerus to behold
candidates.note: Ahasuerus is the Persian king of the Book of Esther, often identified with Xerxes, famous in scripture for a vast empire and overwhelming royal majesty. Melville gives the name to Daggoo, the towering African harpooneer, casting him as an imperial figure of grandeur rather than a servant.

candidates.anchor: son of the Prince of the Powers of the Air
candidates.note: The title echoes Ephesians 2:2, where Satan is called the prince of the power of the air. Melville hangs it on Tashtego, the Gay Head harpooneer, imagining that a Puritan eye might have read so wild and free a figure as something devilish, the period's reflex of casting the non-Christian outsider as demonic.

candidates.anchor: He was as particular about the comfortable arrangement of his part of the boat, as an old stage-driver is about the snugness of his box.
candidates.note: The stage-driver comparison makes Stubb's boatmanship comic and practical. He manages danger by making his working space feel familiar.

candidates.anchor: nothing above hatches was seen of Captain Ahab
candidates.note: Ahab enters through absence and bodily evidence before he fully appears. The ivory leg makes the whale encounter visible before the story explains it.

candidates.anchor: his eyes like powder-pans
candidates.note: Stubb reads Ahab's eyes like explosive gear. The image makes the captain's mood feel dangerous before Ahab fully explains himself.

candidates.anchor: Lighting the pipe at the binnacle lamp and planting the stool on the weather side of the deck, he sat and smoked.
candidates.note: The pipe should mark ordinary rest, but Ahab cannot settle into it. Giving it up shows obsession crowding out even small habits of comfort.

candidates.anchor: my pipe! hard must it go with me if thy charm be gone!
candidates.note: Ahab giving up the pipe shows ordinary comfort being stripped away by obsession. Even a small habit can no longer survive his purpose.

candidates.anchor: It is some systematized exhibition of the whale in his broad genera, that I would now fain put before you.
candidates.note: Ishmael admits that he wants to display the whale systematically. The ambition matters even when the system later looks comic or strained.

candidates.anchor: The classification of the constituents of a chaos, nothing less is here essayed.
candidates.note: The phrase names the problem of the whole chapter: Ishmael tries to classify a subject that feels too large and chaotic to fit any system.

candidates.anchor: Literally this word means Fat-Cutter; usage, however, in time made it equivalent to Chief Harpooneer.
candidates.note: The word Specksnyder preserves an older whaling labor hierarchy. Defining it shows that shipboard power depends on specialized work, not only on captains.

candidates.anchor: the first lives aft, the last forward
candidates.note: Whaling has its own labor geography. Officers and ordinary crew literally live in different parts of the ship.

candidates.anchor: grand political maxim of the sea
candidates.note: The chapter treats ship rank as a political system. Whaling hierarchy is not only practical; it organizes authority, space, and obedience.

candidates.anchor: From his complete inattention to the tidings, you would think that moody Ahab had not heard his menial.
candidates.note: Ahab's indifference at dinner shows command as isolation. Even ordinary shipboard routines become tense when the captain refuses common social signals.

candidates.anchor: the smooth, medallion-shaped tablet
candidates.note: Ahab uses the ivory leg as a writing surface. The detail makes his injured body part of the ship's command system.

candidates.anchor: I take it, that the earliest standers of mastheads were the old Egyptians; because, in all my researches, I find none prior to them.
candidates.note: The Egyptian comparison is comic overreach, but it shows Ishmael's method: even a shipboard job becomes part of a huge cultural history.

candidates.anchor: bill of fare is immutable
candidates.note: The ship becomes a sealed world where even food feels fixed. Ishmael turns ordinary provisions into a sign of life cut off from shore.

candidates.anchor: the unaccompanied, secluded White Whale
candidates.note: The whale is built into a legend of isolation before readers ever see him. Moby Dick becomes a story circulating at sea before he becomes a visible animal.

candidates.anchor: there is somebody down in the after-hold
candidates.note: The rumor of someone hidden below deck turns the Pequod into a ship with secrets. Suspicion arrives before Fedallah fully appears.

candidates.anchor: economical with your lamps and candles
candidates.note: Ishmael turns whale oil into ordinary light, reminding readers that comfort and illumination come from dangerous labor.

candidates.anchor: collateral prosecution of the voyage
candidates.note: Ahab's private revenge is still attached to the official business of the ship. The chapter keeps profit, labor, and obsession in the same frame.

candidates.anchor: A rumpled Chinese jacket of black cotton funereally invested him, with wide black trousers of the same dark stuff.
candidates.note: Fedallah's clothing is described as dark and funereal before the crew understands his role. Melville lets costume work like an omen.

candidates.anchor: casting loose the tackles and bands
candidates.note: The first lowering begins with gear being released, not with heroic action. The practical choreography makes the scene feel charged before the boats are fully away.

candidates.anchor: Fedallah
candidates.note: Ahab's hidden boat crew shows that he has prepared a private mission inside the public voyage. Fedallah becomes part of the book's secrecy before he becomes fully legible.

candidates.anchor: some plumed and glittering god uprising from the sea
candidates.note: The crew reads the sight as an omen before it can become ordinary whale sign. The chapter makes perception itself part of the voyage's danger.

candidates.anchor: skeleton of a stranded walrus
candidates.note: The Albatross is described like a sea corpse, which makes the failed meeting feel eerie before anyone understands its warning.

candidates.unit_title: Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes
candidates.anchor: I know of only four published outlines of the great Sperm Whale; Colnett’s, Huggins’s, Frederick Cuvier’s, and Beale’s.
candidates.note: Ishmael's list of published whale outlines shows his standards for evidence. He wants better pictures, but he still argues like a whaleman correcting books.

candidates.unit_title: Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars
candidates.anchor: Any time these ten years, they tell me, has that man held up that picture, and exhibited that stump to an incredulous world.
candidates.note: The man with the picture and the stump turns whale evidence into public spectacle. Injury, proof, and performance all become part of whaling lore.

candidates.unit_title: Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars
candidates.anchor: the tragic scene in which he lost his leg
candidates.note: The picture turns a whaleman's injury into public display. The moment links bodily evidence, spectacle, and whaling lore.

candidates.anchor: Ere forgetfulness altogether came over me, I had noticed that the seamen at the main and mizzenmast-heads were already drowsy.
candidates.note: The drowsy mastheads show how routine can dull attention even in dangerous work. Melville lets labor fatigue shape the action.

candidates.anchor: For one, I cannot bawl very heartily and work very recklessly at one and the same time.
candidates.note: The Dart turns hunting technique into a lesson about attention. Good whaling requires bodily control, timing, and judgment, not only bravery.

candidates.anchor: the harpooneer is expected to pull his oar meanwhile to the uttermost
candidates.note: The harpooneer is overloaded at the crucial moment: rowing, balancing, timing, and throwing. Melville turns heroic action back into labor.

candidates.anchor: headsman or whale-killer
candidates.note: The chapter argues that the harpooneer is asked to do too much at once. Melville turns hunting technique into a labor critique.

candidates.anchor: But by those clanking links, the vast corpse itself, not the ship, is to be moored.
candidates.note: The corpse is moored to the ship like cargo and danger at once. After the kill, the whale remains an enormous practical problem.

candidates.anchor: It is upon record, that three centuries ago the tongue of the Right Whale was esteemed a great delicacy in France, and commanded large prices there.
candidates.note: The right-whale tongue example turns appetite into history. Ishmael's food chapter asks how cultures decide which parts of a whale become acceptable use.

candidates.anchor: the whale would by all hands be considered a noble dish
candidates.note: Ishmael shows that taste is cultural, not natural. Whale meat can be framed as food rather than only as a dead animal.

candidates.anchor: For that business is an exceedingly laborious one; is not very soon completed; and requires all hands to set about it.
candidates.note: The business after the kill is slow, collective, and exhausting. This sentence reminds students that whaling violence continues as labor.

candidates.anchor: every sailor a butcher
candidates.note: The hunt is only the start of the work. Cutting-in shows the whale becoming a processed commodity through coordinated shipboard labor.

candidates.anchor: Do you not marvel, then, at Stubb’s boast, that he demanded but ten minutes to behead a sperm whale?
candidates.note: Stubb's boast about beheading turns whale anatomy into skilled speed. The severed head then becomes a riddle for Ahab, not only a work product.

candidates.anchor: scientific anatomical feat
candidates.note: The beheading is described like a precise anatomical operation. Whaling labor becomes technical, almost surgical, while remaining violent.

candidates.anchor: It is much the same with him who endeavors the description of the scene.
candidates.note: Ishmael compares describing the work to doing the work. The sentence turns narrative itself into a tangled rope problem.

candidates.unit_title: Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him
candidates.anchor: But we must let it continue hanging there a while till we can get a chance to attend to it.
candidates.note: The hanging head keeps unfinished labor beside the ship. Even when the plot turns elsewhere, the whale's body remains a practical problem.

candidates.anchor: this green, barnacled thing
candidates.note: The local names for parts of the right-whale head turn specialist experience into vocabulary. Ishmael is translating what whalemen notice for readers who have never seen it.

candidates.anchor: the Greenlanders call the
candidates.note: The chapter shows that whalemen name the same body part differently depending on where and how they work.

candidates.anchor: battering-ram power
candidates.note: The sperm whale's head is imagined as a blunt force structure. Melville turns anatomy into an explanation of destructive power.

candidates.anchor: forever remain an infidel as to one of the most appalling
candidates.note: Ishmael turns anatomy into persuasion. The chapter is technical, but its real work is convincing readers that whale violence can be credible.

candidates.anchor: He has carried with him a light tackle called a whip, consisting of only two parts, travelling through a single-sheaved block.
candidates.note: The whip is a simple hoisting rig, but the task is dangerous because it happens inside the whale's head. The tool makes the later accident legible.

candidates.anchor: Securing this block, so that it hangs down from the yardarm, he swings one end of the rope, till it is caught and firmly held by a hand on deck.
candidates.note: The rope-and-block sequence makes the rescue scene depend on practical coordination. Every fastening and hand matters before Tashtego falls.

candidates.anchor: the deep cistern will yield no more
candidates.note: The whale's head is treated like a well that can run dry. Extraction becomes technical, bodily, and strangely domestic at once.

candidates.anchor: a clean one
candidates.note: The joke means the ship has no oil, not that it is morally pure or physically tidy. Whaling slang turns economic failure into comedy.

candidates.unit_title: The Honor and Glory of Whaling
candidates.anchor: Those were the knightly days of our profession, when we only bore arms to succor the distressed, and not to fill men’s lamp-feeders.
candidates.note: Ishmael turns whaling into mock chivalry. The joke matters because it shows how hard he works to dignify a bloody trade.

candidates.anchor: one seventh or Sunday of his time
candidates.note: The Sunday fraction makes whale breathing feel both measurable and odd. Ishmael turns anatomy into a comparison with human weekly rhythm.

candidates.anchor: This rampart is pierced by several sally-ports for the convenience of ships and whales; conspicuous among which are the straits of Sunda and Malacca.
candidates.note: The Sunda and Malacca straits place whale movement inside a real global seascape. The herd scene is also a geography lesson.

candidates.anchor: Perhaps the only formal whaling code authorized by legislative enactment, was that of Holland.
candidates.note: Ishmael treats whaling custom as a legal problem. The chapter uses rules about captured whales to ask who gets to claim property and power.

candidates.anchor: ambergris is a very curious substance
candidates.note: Ambergris is valuable because it moves from whale body to luxury trade. The chapter turns a disgusting source into perfume economics.

candidates.anchor: Schmerenburgh or Smeerenberg
candidates.note: The old Dutch whaling settlement helps explain the joke about whale smell. Melville ties odor, history, and whale-processing geography together.

candidates.anchor: ship-keepers
candidates.note: Ship-keepers are crew left aboard while the boats chase whales. Pip's trauma begins inside an ordinary work assignment, which makes the accident feel cruelly routine.

candidates.anchor: It was our business to squeeze these lumps back into fluid.
candidates.note: The squeezing work turns whale processing into communal touch. The chapter is strange because labor becomes almost utopian feeling.

candidates.anchor: Constantine’s bath
candidates.note: The tub name shows how whaling work develops its own jargon and jokes. The scene is technical as well as sensual.

candidates.anchor: There they lay in their triangular oaken vaults, each mariner a chiselled muteness; a score of lamps flashing upon his hooded eyes.
candidates.note: The sailors sleep by whale-oil light, so the hunt returns as ordinary comfort. The chapter quietly connects danger at sea to warmth below deck.

candidates.anchor: This done, the hatches are replaced, and hermetically closed, like a closet walled up.
candidates.note: The sealed hatches make extraction look orderly after chaos. The ship has to contain what it has taken from the whale.

candidates.anchor: Commanded by a naval Post-Captain, the Rattler made a rattling voyage of it, and did some service; how much does not appear.
candidates.note: The Rattler detail keeps Enderby history lively rather than abstract. Ishmael turns whaling commerce into adventure record.

candidates.anchor: In 1819, the same house fitted out a discovery whale ship of their own, to go on a tasting cruise to the remote waters of Japan.
candidates.note: The discovery ship shows whaling tied to exploration and long-distance routes. Commerce and geography move together in Ishmael's industry history.

candidates.anchor: first English ships that ever regularly hunted the Sperm Whale
candidates.note: The Enderby dates turn whaling into commercial history rather than background scenery. Ishmael places the Pequod's voyage inside a larger industry of ships, firms, and distant hunting grounds.

candidates.unit_title: Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?
candidates.anchor: Of all the pre-adamite whales yet exhumed, by far the largest is the Alabama one mentioned in the last chapter, and that was less than seventy feet in length in the skeleton.
candidates.note: The Alabama fossil lets Ishmael compare ancient whales to living ones. Extinction and survival enter the whaling argument.

candidates.anchor: ivory leg had received a half-splintering shock
candidates.note: Ahab's injury keeps returning through objects, repairs, and command. His body is part of the ship's material system, not separate from it.

candidates.anchor: thousand nameless mechanical emergencies
candidates.note: The carpenter's value comes from practical intelligence under pressure, the kind of skill the ship needs constantly but rarely celebrates.

candidates.unit_title: Ahab and Starbuck in the Cabin
candidates.anchor: casks below must have sprung a bad leak
candidates.note: The leaking oil creates a direct conflict between Starbuck's duty to the ship and Ahab's refusal to pause the hunt.

candidates.anchor: Nevertheless, this old man’s was a patient hammer wielded by a patient arm.
candidates.note: Perth's patient hammer turns grief into craft. His suffering is not decorative; it shapes the work Ahab will use.

candidates.anchor: In no Paradise myself, I am impatient of all misery in others that is not mad.
candidates.note: Ahab can tolerate suffering only when it becomes fury or purpose. Perth's quieter misery unsettles him because it refuses that pattern.

candidates.anchor: corpusants
candidates.note: Corpusants are storm lights seen by sailors, but the crew reads them as omens. The scene lets natural event and symbolic terror occupy the same deck.

candidates.anchor: The strong, unstaggering breeze abounded so, that sky and air seemed vast outbellying sails; the whole world boomed before the wind.
candidates.note: The vast wind image makes the whole world seem to push the ship forward. Ahab reads motion as permission.

candidates.anchor: The wooden reel and angular log attached hung, long untouched, just beneath the railing of the after bulwarks.
candidates.note: The untouched log-line shows a guidance tool neglected until late. Broken measurement becomes part of the voyage's unraveling.

candidates.anchor: all the elements had combined to rot
candidates.note: The rotted log-line shows how a neglected knowledge tool fails when it is finally needed. After Ahab rejects other instruments, broken measurement becomes part of the voyage's unraveling.

candidates.anchor: Yet the grey Manxman⁠—the oldest mariner of all⁠—declared that the wild thrilling sounds that were heard, were the voices of newly drowned men in the sea.
candidates.note: The Manxman's explanation turns sound into omen. The chapter makes the sea seem full of voices from the dead.

candidates.anchor: coffin laid upon two line-tubs
candidates.note: The coffin now sits among whaling gear. Melville keeps folding symbol back into practical shipboard use.

candidates.anchor: It domineered above them so, that all their bodings, doubts, misgivings, fears, were fain to hide beneath their souls, and not sprout forth a single spear or leaf.
candidates.note: The whale's imagined nearness suppresses every doubt on deck. Moby Dick dominates the crew before he fully appears.

candidates.anchor: most miserably misnamed the Delight
candidates.note: The Delight's warning does not make Ahab reconsider. He folds another ship's disaster back into the logic of the chase.

candidates.anchor: But though thus contrasting within, the contrast was only in shades and shadows without; those two seemed one; it was only the sex, as it were, that distinguished them.
candidates.note: Ishmael describes contrast and unity at the same time: sea and sky seem separate, then almost one. The calm matters because it comes just before catastrophe.

candidates.anchor: he travels faster than I thought for;⁠—the topgallant sails!⁠—aye, they should have been kept on her all night.
candidates.note: The topgallant order shows Ahab converting frustration into more sail. Every delay becomes another demand for speed.

candidates.anchor: Towards thee I roll
candidates.note: Ahab's final speech turns pursuit into total self-definition. He can recognize destruction and still choose to aim himself at it.

candidates.anchor: summerhouse to the angels
candidates.note: The image briefly makes creation seem bright and welcoming. The final chase feels harsher because this glimpse of beauty appears and vanishes.

candidates.anchor: Round and round, then, and ever contracting towards the button-like black bubble at the axis of that slowly wheeling circle, like another Ixion I did revolve.
candidates.note: The circling image places Ishmael at the edge of death before rescue. Survival begins inside the same whirlpool that ends the ship.

candidates.anchor: long Vaticans and street-stalls of the earth
candidates.note: The Extracts build a whale scrapbook from sacred texts, travel writing, science, jokes, and literature. That mixed archive prepares readers for a novel that keeps changing genres.

candidates.anchor:
“I saw the opening maw of hell,

 With endless pains and sorrows there;

 Which none but they that feel can tell⁠—

 Oh, I was plunging to despair.
candidates.note: The hymn gives the sermon emotional force before its argument arrives. Despair, punishment, and rescue are already in the room.

candidates.anchor: Isolatoes
candidates.note: Ishmael's invented word means people who live as if on separate islands. On the Pequod, it captures the ship's strange mix of solitude and forced fellowship.

candidates.anchor: Some days elapsed
candidates.note: The chapter title sounds like stage direction, and the scene plays like a tense exchange between authority and comic resistance.

candidates.anchor: Stubb solus
candidates.note: The Latin stage direction marks the chapter as theater. Stubb's private voice lets readers hear how Ahab's command keeps working on the crew.

candidates.anchor: natural verity of the main points
candidates.note: The chapter keeps asking for trust, but it also shows how much proof depends on the storyteller's authority.

candidates.anchor: Goney (Albatross)
candidates.note: The failed meeting with the Albatross makes communication at sea feel fragile. A gam can bring news, but this one turns into silence and missed warning.

candidates.anchor: As told at the Golden Inn
candidates.note: The heading signals a story inside a story. Moby-Dick keeps slipping into narrated frames, so readers have to notice who is telling and where.

candidates.anchor: whole round sea was one huge cheese
candidates.note: The grotesque joke turns horror into an image readers can picture, making the shark scene funny and repulsive at the same time.

candidates.anchor: where, I should like to know, will you obtain a better chance to study practical cetology than here?
candidates.note: The suspended whale heads become a live classroom. Ishmael turns the deck into a comparative cetology demonstration.

candidates.anchor: “What has he in his hand there?” cried Starbuck, pointing to something wavingly held by the German.
candidates.note: Starbuck's eye catches the object in the German captain's hand before anyone explains it. The small visual cue starts the chapter's bargaining and embarrassment.

candidates.unit_title: The Honor and Glory of Whaling
candidates.anchor: careful disorderliness
candidates.note: Ishmael's heroic history of whaling is intentionally disorderly. The chapter celebrates the profession while making its evidence feel comic and unstable.

candidates.anchor: all fountains must run wine today
candidates.note: Stubb's brag turns the strike into holiday language, letting the chapter mix skill, excess, and comic bravado.

candidates.anchor: These laws might be engraven
candidates.note: Melville makes the legal code tiny on purpose, mocking how huge and slippery ownership law can become.

candidates.anchor: And though now nailed amidst all the rustiness of iron bolts and the verdigris of copper spikes, yet, untouchable and immaculate to any foulness, it still preserved its Quito glow.
candidates.note: The bright coin stands apart from the worn mast hardware. Its power is visual before it is interpretive: everyone can see that Ahab has made it special.

candidates.anchor: For it was set apart and sanctified to one awe-striking end; and however wanton in their sailor ways, one and all, the mariners revered it as the white whale’s talisman.
candidates.note: The coin is treated like a sacred object because Ahab has assigned it one purpose. The crew reads it through that pressure.

candidates.anchor: Trumpet to mouth, the old man was standing in his hoisted quarter-boat, his ivory leg plainly revealed to the stranger captain, who was carelessly reclining in his own boat’s bow.
candidates.note: Ahab and Boomer meet as two captains marked by whale injury. The scene makes their different responses visible before either man argues about Moby Dick.

candidates.anchor: That is hard which should be soft, and that is soft which should be hard.
candidates.note: Ahab turns bodily repair into a riddle about what should be hard or soft. The carpenter hears materials; Ahab hears metaphysics.

candidates.anchor: Bachelor
candidates.note: The Bachelor is cheerful, successful, and homeward-bound. That contrast makes the Pequod's course look chosen rather than inevitable.

candidates.anchor: nakedness of unrelieved radiance
candidates.note: The sky's brightness makes navigation feel hostile rather than clarifying. Ahab faces a world that gives light without guidance.

candidates.unit_title: The Deck Towards the End of the First Night Watch
candidates.anchor:
If I had skysail poles, I’d sway them up now.”

 “Sir!⁠—in God’s name!⁠—sir?”

 “Well.”

 “The anchors are working, sir.
candidates.note: Ahab's demand for more sail turns weather into defiance. Starbuck hears danger; Ahab hears an opportunity to press harder.

candidates.unit_title: The Deck Towards the End of the First Night Watch
candidates.anchor: Ahab standing by the helm
candidates.note: The late short chapters use stage-like compression. A few commands can show the whole ship tightening under Ahab's will.

candidates.anchor: Stubb and Flask mounted on them
candidates.note: The crew's talk at the bulwarks gives readers a lower-deck angle on the storm. Melville keeps returning to collective voices, not only Ahab's.

candidates.anchor: Delight
candidates.note: The ship's name is bitterly ironic because its news is grim. The last gam functions like a final warning before the chase.

candidates.anchor: Much was I disappointed upon learning that the little packet for Nantucket had already sailed, and that no way of reaching that place would offer, till the following Monday.
candidates.note: Missing the packet forces Ishmael into New Bedford and into the comic ordeal of finding a bed. The delay is what makes the Queequeg encounter possible.

candidates.anchor: my old carpetbag
candidates.note: Ishmael travels light, poor, and alone. The small bag keeps the opening grounded in ordinary vulnerability before the book expands into myth.

candidates.anchor: A boggy, soggy, squitchy picture truly, enough to drive a nervous man distracted.
candidates.note: The inn painting is deliberately hard to read. It trains students for a novel where images of whales, ships, and danger often arrive blurred before they become meaningful.

candidates.anchor: When I was a child, I well remember a somewhat similar circumstance that befell me; whether it was a reality or a dream, I never could entirely settle.
candidates.note: Ishmael links waking beside Queequeg to a childhood memory of fear and uncertainty. The scene makes intimacy feel strange before it becomes trust.

candidates.anchor: And the man that has anything bountifully laughable about him, be sure there is more in that man than you perhaps think for.
candidates.note: The silent breakfast makes the inn's rough community visible. Ishmael notices laughter because humor becomes one way sailors survive danger and embarrassment.

candidates.anchor: Returning from my first morning stroll, I again sallied out upon this special errand.
candidates.note: Ishmael's errand to the chapel moves the story from lodging-house comedy into mourning. Before the Pequod sails, the book makes shipwreck and loss visible.

candidates.anchor: a Socratic wisdom
candidates.note: Socrates was the Athenian philosopher of the fifth century BC who taught by relentless questioning and claimed that real wisdom begins in knowing how little one knows. Ishmael credits Queequeg with that same calm, unschooled depth, ranking the supposed savage with the founder of Western philosophy.

candidates.anchor: we felt very nice and snug
candidates.note: The chapter pauses the plot for bodily comfort and trust. That warmth makes the later shipboard isolation feel less like the book's only possible world.

candidates.anchor: His father was a High Chief, a King; his uncle a High Priest; and on the maternal side he boasted aunts who were the wives of unconquerable warriors.
candidates.note: Queequeg's royal family history pushes back against treating him as a curiosity. Ishmael's account is imperfect, but it gives Queequeg status and a past.

candidates.anchor: clam or cod
candidates.note: The chowder chapter is comic, but it also slows the book around food, appetite, and port comfort before the Pequod's danger takes over.

candidates.anchor: “What do you mean by that, Captain Peleg?” said I, now jumping on the bulwarks, and leaving my comrade standing on the wharf.
candidates.note: Ishmael's quick defense of Queequeg makes their friendship public. He has moved from curious observer to companion willing to answer for him.

candidates.anchor:
“I mean,” he replied, “he must show his papers.”

 “Yes,” said Captain Bildad in his hollow voice, sticking his head from behind Peleg’s, out of the wigwam.
candidates.note: Peleg and Bildad demand papers, but Queequeg's worth is proved through action and skill. The scene tests what kinds of evidence count aboard a whaleship.

candidates.anchor: his mark
candidates.note: Queequeg's signature turns friendship, religion, literacy, and labor contract into one scene. The chapter asks who gets recognized as trustworthy in a commercial world.

candidates.anchor: Going Aboard
candidates.note: The departure is full of darkness, confusion, and half-seen figures. Melville makes boarding the ship feel like crossing into a different moral climate.

candidates.anchor: And, as for Captain Ahab, no sign of him was yet to be seen; only, they said he was in the cabin.
candidates.note: Ahab's absence keeps him powerful. Even before readers meet him fully, the cabin becomes a hidden center of pressure.

candidates.anchor: one touch of land
candidates.note: Land usually promises safety, but Bulkington's chapter reverses that comfort. Here, shore can mean danger and open sea can mean truth.

candidates.anchor: S.W.F. (Sperm Whale Fishery)
candidates.note: The abbreviation makes the Sperm Whale Fishery sound like a formal credential. Ishmael uses the joke to defend work that polite society may find absurd.

candidates.anchor: Yale College and my Harvard
candidates.note: Ishmael claims whaling educated him, so the voyage becomes a school of thought as well as work.

candidates.anchor: afraid of a whale
candidates.note: Starbuck's courage includes fear. He respects the whale as a real danger, so his caution becomes a professional virtue rather than cowardice.

candidates.anchor: Good-humored, easy, and careless, he presided over his whaleboat as if the most deadly encounter were but a dinner, and his crew all invited guests.
candidates.note: Stubb's cheerfulness is not innocence; it is a working style inside danger. His whaleboat feels social even when the work is deadly.

candidates.anchor: Yes, their supreme lord and dictator was there, though hitherto unseen by any eyes not permitted to penetrate into the now sacred retreat of the cabin.
candidates.note: Ahab's first full appearance is framed as rule before personality. The language of lordship and dictatorship makes command feel almost sacred and threatening.

candidates.anchor: The warmly cool, clear, ringing, perfumed, overflowing, redundant days, were as crystal goblets of Persian sherbet, heaped up⁠—flaked up, with rose-water snow.
candidates.note: The beautiful weather sharpens the ugliness of the command conflict. Calm surfaces do not prevent tension aboard the Pequod.

candidates.anchor: Ahab seemed a pyramid, and I, like a blazing fool, kept kicking at it.
candidates.note: Stubb's dream makes Ahab feel monumental and absurd at once. Comedy becomes a way to speak fear without openly challenging command.

candidates.anchor: queer dream
candidates.note: Stubb's dream keeps the book's warnings in a comic register. Even jokes and dreams become part of the prophecy system around Ahab.

candidates.anchor: the seven Imperial Electors
candidates.note: The seven Imperial Electors were the German princes who, under the Golden Bull of 1356, held the sole right to elect the Holy Roman Emperor and dined with him at his coronation feast. Melville reaches for that stiff, ceremonious banquet to picture the frozen formality of Ahab presiding in silence over his three mates at the cabin table.

candidates.anchor: But presently, catching hold of the mizzen shrouds, he swings himself to the deck, and in an even, unexhilarated voice, saying, “Dinner, Mr.
candidates.note: The movement from rigging to dinner table makes hierarchy physical. Ahab enters the shared space, but the scene still keeps him emotionally apart.

candidates.anchor: Now, as the business of standing mastheads, ashore or afloat, is a very ancient and interesting one, let us in some measure expatiate here.
candidates.note: Ishmael treats masthead duty as practical lookout work and as an old human habit of watching from heights. A shipboard job becomes part of a larger pattern.

candidates.anchor: after the squall
candidates.note: Ishmael reconstructs Ahab's private labor instead of simply reporting it. The chapter reminds readers how much of the captain is inferred rather than directly seen.

candidates.anchor: vast practical joke
candidates.note: Ishmael turns mortal danger into a joke about paperwork and wills. The laughter is defensive: comedy becomes a way to live with risk.

candidates.unit_title: Of the Monstrous Pictures of Whales
candidates.anchor: Now, by all odds, the most ancient extant portrait anyways purporting to be the whale’s, is to be found in the famous cavern-pagoda of Elephanta, in India.
candidates.note: The Elephanta example pushes whale imagery into global and ancient settings, while also showing how easily pictures become distorted evidence.

candidates.unit_title: Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes
candidates.anchor: In the previous chapter Colnett and Cuvier have been referred to.
candidates.note: The return to Colnett and Cuvier makes the picture chapters cumulative. Ishmael keeps comparing authorities because no single image settles the whale.

candidates.anchor: vast meadows of brit
candidates.note: Brit is the tiny food that draws right whales into feeding grounds. The chapter makes whale magnitude depend on small, almost invisible life.

candidates.anchor: For leagues and leagues it undulated round us, so that we seemed to be sailing through boundless fields of ripe and golden wheat.
candidates.note: The wheat-field image makes whale feeding beautiful before it becomes technical. Students can see the feeding ground first as a scene, then as a system.

candidates.anchor: lonely, alluring jet
candidates.note: The squid is mistaken for a whale sign, then becomes a sign of uncertainty instead. At sea, seeing something does not always mean knowing what it means.

candidates.anchor: the great live squid
candidates.note: The squid reminds readers that the whale hunt sits inside a stranger marine world. The ocean is not just backdrop for Ahab's desire.

candidates.anchor: At first sight, you would not think it so strong as it really is.
candidates.note: The whale-line looks deceptively slight, which is why the chapter keeps stressing danger. A small-looking tool can govern life and death in the boat.

candidates.anchor: the magical, sometimes horrible whale-line
candidates.note: The whale-line is essential gear and a deadly hazard. Melville makes an ordinary rope carry the whole risk of the chase.

candidates.anchor: It was my turn to stand at the foremast-head; and with my shoulders leaning against the slackened royal shrouds, to and fro I idly swayed in what seemed an enchanted air.
candidates.note: Ishmael's dreamy masthead mood makes the sudden whale action feel almost enchanted. The hunt begins from drowsiness, not heroic readiness.

candidates.anchor: The crotch alluded to on a previous page deserves independent mention.
candidates.note: The crotch pauses over a small tool because small tools organize the whole chase. The chapter asks readers to respect technical detail.

candidates.anchor: notched stick
candidates.note: The crotch is a small tool, but it controls how spare harpoons wait for use. The chapter shows how whaling depends on specialized equipment.

candidates.anchor: a notched stick of a peculiar form
candidates.note: The crotch is small boat hardware, but it matters because the harpoon must be ready before danger arrives. Tools shape the hunt before the strike.

candidates.anchor: Such an unwonted bustle was he in that the staid Starbuck, his official superior, quietly resigned to him for the time the sole management of affairs.
candidates.note: Stubb's bustle gives him temporary command over the aftermath. His comic energy is also a way of managing shipboard work.

candidates.anchor: feed upon the creature that feeds his lamp
candidates.note: The chapter makes whale consumption comic and uneasy. Ishmael can describe appetite, but he keeps showing how strange it is to turn the whale into food.

candidates.anchor: You would have thought we were offering up ten thousand red oxen to the sea gods.
candidates.note: The sea-gods image makes cutting-in feel ritualized and excessive. Industrial work is described with sacrificial language.

candidates.anchor: My original opinion remains unchanged; but it is only an opinion.
candidates.note: Ishmael's confidence stops at opinion. The skin chapter sounds scientific, but it openly admits uncertainty.

candidates.anchor: I use for marks in my whale-books
candidates.note: Ishmael studies whales with specimens and notes, not only with books. The detail makes his research practical and hands-on.

candidates.anchor: For hours and hours from the almost stationary ship that hideous sight is seen.
candidates.note: The floating carcass becomes a lingering public sight. The chapter makes the aftermath of extraction ugly and hard to look away from.

candidates.anchor: There is no staying in any one place; for at one and the same time everything has to be done everywhere.
candidates.note: The monkey-rope scene is chaotic because the work happens all at once. Melville makes interdependence feel like physical disorder.

candidates.unit_title: Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him
candidates.anchor: gamboge ghost of a Fedallah
candidates.note: Gamboge is a deep saffron-yellow pigment derived from Southeast Asian tree resin, used as a watercolor paint and a harsh purgative medicine. Flask applies the color-word to Fedallah's complexion, coding his South Asian appearance as sickly and spectral — a moment of casual racial caricature that also marks Fedallah as uncanny.

candidates.unit_title: Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him
candidates.anchor: For the present other matters press, and the best we can do now for the head, is to pray heaven the tackles may hold.
candidates.note: The hanging head is both symbol and equipment problem. Melville makes the superstition depend on ordinary tackle strong enough to hold a dangerous weight.

candidates.unit_title: Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him
candidates.anchor: prodigious head
candidates.note: Stubb and Flask want a right-whale head to balance the Pequod. Practical superstition turns anatomy into shipboard strategy.

candidates.anchor: To the Nantucketer, they present the two extremes of all the known varieties of the whale.
candidates.note: For the Nantucketer, whale classification begins in use and encounter. The heads matter because whalemen know them through work.

candidates.anchor: In the first place, you are struck by the general contrast between these heads.
candidates.note: The chapter asks readers to look comparatively. Sperm whale and right whale anatomy become two ways of organizing knowledge.

candidates.anchor: grey-headed whale
candidates.note: The phrase treats age as something whalemen can read from the body. Ishmael turns the whale's head into a record of experience, not just anatomy.

candidates.anchor: Two hundred years ago an old Dutch voyager likened its shape to that of a shoemaker’s last.
candidates.note: The shoemaker's-last comparison makes right-whale anatomy ordinary and strange at once. Ishmael teaches readers to picture the head through familiar objects.

candidates.anchor: Nor is this precious substance found unalloyed in any other part of the creature.
candidates.note: The valuable spermaceti is difficult to reach and easy to waste. Anatomy becomes an extraction problem, not just a description of body parts.

candidates.anchor: In the full-grown creature the skull will measure at least twenty feet in length.
candidates.note: The skull measurement makes the whale's head feel architectural. Scale itself becomes part of the chapter's argument.

candidates.anchor: He seemed to be working in obedience to some particular presentiment.
candidates.note: Stubb's presentiment makes technique feel ominous. Even a practical hunting method enters the book's atmosphere of signs.

candidates.anchor: It became imperative to lance the flying whale, or be content to lose him
candidates.note: Pitchpoling answers a practical problem: how to strike a whale moving too fast for ordinary approach. The technique makes chase speed visible.

candidates.anchor: Let us, then, look at this matter, along with some interesting items contingent.
candidates.note: The chapter begins as an inquiry rather than a settled lesson. Ishmael asks readers to treat the whale's spout as evidence, while admitting that evidence has limits.

candidates.anchor: Wherefore the necessity for his periodical visits to the upper world.
candidates.note: The whale's need to surface ties mystery to biology. The spout is symbolic, but it starts from breath and bodily necessity.

candidates.anchor: the whale can only live by inhaling the disengaged air in the open atmosphere
candidates.note: The chapter tries to explain the whale's breath and spout. A visible spray becomes a scientific question.

candidates.anchor: The compact round body of its root expands into two broad, firm, flat palms or flukes, gradually shoaling away to less than an inch in thickness.
candidates.note: The tail description translates motion into anatomy. Ishmael wants students to see power in shape before seeing it in action.

candidates.anchor: At the crotch or junction, these flukes slightly overlap, then sideways recede from each other like wings, leaving a wide vacancy between.
candidates.note: The flukes are described with nearly mechanical care. That physical detail grounds the tail's later symbolic force in structure and motion.

candidates.anchor: They generally are of two sorts; those composed almost entirely of females, and those mustering none but young vigorous males, or bulls, as they are familiarly designated.
candidates.note: Ishmael divides whale groups by sex and age, then borrows human social language to explain them. The passage mixes observation with projection.

candidates.anchor: known as schools
candidates.note: The chapter treats whales as social groups with patterns and leaders. Ishmael turns whale behavior into a kind of marine sociology.

candidates.anchor: For at that time, and indeed until a comparatively late day, the precise origin of ambergris remained, like amber itself, a problem to the learned.
candidates.note: The uncertain origin of ambergris lets Ishmael mix science, rumor, and commerce. The chapter is about knowledge as much as perfume.

candidates.anchor: No wonder that in old times this sperm was such a favourite cosmetic.
candidates.note: The cosmetic reference links brutal shipboard labor to refinement and luxury. Melville keeps the worlds of extraction and comfort uncomfortably close.

candidates.anchor: And an idol, indeed, it is; or, rather, in old times, its likeness was.
candidates.note: The idol comparison makes the whale part anatomical, sacred, and unsettling at once. The chapter deliberately mixes work, religion, and body.

candidates.anchor: strange, enigmatical object
candidates.note: The whale's skin becomes protective clothing for the mincer. Melville turns butchery into ritual imagery without hiding the physical labor.

candidates.anchor: the only true lamp
candidates.note: Melville contrasts natural sunlight with the deceptive glare of the try-works. The shipboard episode widens into a warning about false illumination.

candidates.anchor: How is it, that you, a mere oarsman in the fishery, pretend to know aught about the subterranean parts of the whale?
candidates.note: Ishmael anticipates a challenge to his authority. The chapter's knowledge claim depends on travel, work, and storytelling credibility.

candidates.anchor: Did erudite Stubb, mounted upon your capstan, deliver lectures on the anatomy of the Cetacea; and by help of the windlass, hold up a specimen rib for exhibition?
candidates.note: The joke about Stubb lecturing anatomy exposes how unlikely Ishmael's knowledge performance can sound. The chapter knows its own absurdity.

candidates.anchor: Fain am I to stagger to this emprise under the weightiest words of the dictionary.
candidates.note: Ishmael jokes that only the heaviest dictionary words can carry fossil whales. Deep time forces the chapter into deliberately oversized language.

candidates.unit_title: Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?
candidates.anchor: Whereas, we have already seen, that the tape-measure gives seventy-two feet for the skeleton of a large sized modern whale.
candidates.note: The tape-measure brings speculation back to physical evidence. Ishmael's question about whale decline depends on size, records, and uncertainty.

candidates.anchor: smooth, slow heaving swells
candidates.note: The sea's beauty gilds danger without removing it. This quiet chapter matters because it shows why the voyage can still seduce the senses.

candidates.anchor: sun and whale both stilly died together
candidates.note: The dying whale turns toward the sun, and Ahab reads the scene through his own hunger for meaning. Animal death becomes another symbolic mirror.

candidates.anchor: Marine Insurance companies
candidates.note: Stubb's joke points to the business of pricing risk at sea. The banter is comic, but it rests on maritime commerce.

candidates.anchor: What’s the use of thunder
candidates.note: The question cuts the storm down to a worker's immediate discomfort. Ahab may read omens, but the sailor aloft feels weather as labor.

candidates.anchor: preventer tackles
candidates.note: Preventer tackles are extra rigging used to secure control in dangerous conditions. The technical detail helps the storm crisis feel practical, not abstract.

candidates.anchor: Rachel
candidates.note: The Rachel asks Ahab to interrupt his hunt for a human search. His refusal is one of the clearest moral tests in the final movement.

candidates.anchor: Like cures like
candidates.note: Ahab borrows an old medical proverb to explain why Pip steadies him. The phrase helps students see how strange and intimate their bond has become.

candidates.anchor: If thou speakest thus to me much more, Ahab’s purpose keels up in him
candidates.note: Pip's care threatens Ahab's purpose because it awakens a competing loyalty. For a moment, refusing the hunt becomes imaginable.

candidates.anchor: “What d’ye see?” cried Ahab, flattening his face to the sky.
candidates.note: Ahab's question makes sight the first drama of the chase. Everything depends on who sees the whale, and how quickly that sight becomes command.

candidates.anchor: “D’ye see him?” cried Ahab; but the whale was not yet in sight.
candidates.note: Ahab's repeated question narrows the world to sight and pursuit. At the end, everything depends on whether the whale can be seen.

candidates.anchor: Hist! Did you hear that noise, Cabaco?
candidates.note: The overheard voices reveal that Ahab has brought hidden crew aboard. The tiny chapter makes secrecy audible before Fedallah fully enters the plot.

candidates.anchor: using my own hand for the shuttle
candidates.note: The weaving image makes the chapter feel like a miniature version of Melville's craft: the voyage is being woven as it goes.

candidates.anchor: peculiar and not very pleasant smell
candidates.note: The Rose-Bud episode makes profit depend on recognizing value inside disgust. Stubb understands the smell as money before others do.

candidates.anchor: Dough-Boy
candidates.note: The dinner routine turns rank into habit. Who sits, who serves, and who waits shows that shipboard hierarchy is built into ordinary meals.

candidates.anchor: those uncivilized seas mostly frequented by the Sperm Whale fishermen
candidates.note: The phrase places Moby Dick inside a global whaling map, but it also carries colonial language for ocean regions. Ishmael's geography is practical and culturally loaded at once.

candidates.anchor: geometrical circle
candidates.note: The chapter keeps the whale's brain oddly inaccessible. Measurement and dissection do not make the animal fully knowable.

candidates.anchor: illuminated shrine
